explanation_md: `a.size()` returns 4 (the template argument). `.front()` returns the first element (5) and `.back()` returns the last element (20). Each is printed on its own line.

options.a: The loop will iterate one too many times
options.b: `int i` is signed while `a.size()` returns an unsigned type, causing a signed/unsigned comparison warning
options.c: `a.size()` cannot be called in a loop condition
options.d: The array is not initialized so `a.size()` is undefined

explanation_md: `a.size()` returns `std::size_t`, which is an unsigned integer type. Comparing it with the signed `int i` generates a signed/unsigned comparison warning under `-Wextra`. Fix by declaring `i` as `std::size_t`. The loop count and array initialization are both fine.
For each index i, the program prints `v[i] + i`. At i=0: 4+0=4. At i=1: 8+1=9. At i=2: 12+2=14. They are separated by spaces, followed by a newline.

options.b: 5, always — the size is fixed at compile time regardless of initialization
options.c: It depends on how many elements were explicitly listed in the initializer
options.d: `arr.size()` is not available; you must use `sizeof(arr) / sizeof(arr[0])`

explanation_md: `std::array<int, 5>` always has exactly 5 elements. The `{}` initializer zero-initializes those 5 elements but does not change the count. `.size()` always returns the template argument N, which is 5 here.
